And there's other ways that you can signal to. So one thing that we really like nowadays is called a cost cap. Cost caps are known inside of the platforms as cost per result goals. Cost per result goals are awesome. They're exactly as described. They are you defining a range that you're willing to participate in in these auctions. Okay. So every ad platform has advertising auctions. That's how you compete against other advertisers to reach the people that you want to get your ads in front of to convert. When you put a cost cap on the account, otherwise known as a cost per result goal, what you're doing is you're saying, okay, out of the total amount of auctions that are available for me to participate in, I only want to participate in the ones that are below a certain threshold. Right? So as an example, those all little green dots represent auctions. And I say, Hey, this is my target cost. I want $100 cost per result or less. Let's assume that's a cost per call in this example. When you put a cost cap, what you're saying is, Hey, I'm willing to participate in auctions down here. Anything down here clear out for me. But what you're also saying when you do a cost cap is you're willing to have auctions that are a little bit higher than that in an attempt to average out to that cost. Okay. Very core concept as an advertisers to understand the difference between a cost cap and what's known as a bid cap. A bid cap just to refresh your memory. And I'm not trying to talk elementary to you. That's not what I like to do here on this channel. But surprisingly, everybody's gotten used to automated bidding recently. And I've noticed there's actually amongst very high spend advertisers, not a lot of common awareness between cost caps.
